text: Co Packaged Optics (CPO) As AI clusters scale to tens of thousands of GPUs, copper cables between racks can't keep up — too slow, too much heat. CPO replaces copper with laser light by integrating optical engines directly onto the chip package. Why It's Next (Not Now) CPO market projected at $20B by 2036, 37% CAGR Lumentum disclosed an incremental multi h... # Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) As AI clusters scale to tens of thousands of GPUs, copper cables between racks can't keep up — too slow, too much heat. CPO replaces copper with laser light by integrating optical engines directly onto the chip package. ## Why It's Next (Not Now) - CPO market projected at $20B by 2036, 37% CAGR - Lumentum disclosed an incremental multi-hundred-million-dollar CPO order for 1H 2027 delivery - Coherent's March 2026 NVIDIA partnership added multibillion-dollar purchase commitments, future capacity rights, an...
title: Co-Packaged Optics (CPO)

text: EUV Tool Bottleneck EUV lithography is the deepest upstream choke point in AI semiconductors. Once power, land, and near term cleanroom constraints are partially worked around, the limiting factor becomes how many advanced wafers the industry can expose through ASML tools and the tiny supplier chain behind them. Why It Matters ASML remains the only EUV su... # EUV Tool Bottleneck EUV lithography is the deepest upstream choke point in AI semiconductors. Once power, land, and near-term cleanroom constraints are partially worked around, the limiting factor becomes how many advanced wafers the industry can expose through ASML tools and the tiny supplier chain behind them. ## Why It Matters - ASML remains the only EUV supplier. - One gigawatt of Rubin-class capacity was framed as requiring about `2 million` EUV passes and roughly `3.5` EUV tools. - ASML output was framed as roughly `70` to...
title: EUV Tool Bottleneck

text: Memory Supercycle Every AI chip needs memory (DRAM/HBM) to hold the data it's processing. HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) is special memory stacked in layers and bonded directly to the GPU — it's 85% less dense than regular DRAM and much harder to make. Why It's a Bottleneck AI is consuming so much memory that there isn't enough left for phones, PCs, and othe... # Memory Supercycle Every AI chip needs memory (DRAM/HBM) to hold the data it's processing. HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) is special memory stacked in layers and bonded directly to the GPU — it's 85% less dense than regular DRAM and much harder to make. ## Why It's a Bottleneck AI is consuming so much memory that there isn't enough left for phones, PCs, and other devices. The result: - DRAM contract prices surged +90-95% in a single quarter (Q1 2026, TrendForce) - DDR4 spot price ($2.10/Gbit) now exceeds HBM3e ($1.70/Gbit) — unprece...
title: Memory Supercycle

text: N3 Wafer Crunch N3 refers to TSMC's 3 nanometer manufacturing process — the most advanced way to make chips. Every major AI accelerator is moving to N3 simultaneously in 2026, creating a capacity crunch. Why It's a Bottleneck AI already consumes 60% of TSMC N3 output (2026), projected 86% by 2027 N3 utilization at 100%+ in H2 2026 TSMC Q4 2025 printed $33... # N3 Wafer Crunch N3 refers to TSMC's 3-nanometer manufacturing process — the most advanced way to make chips. Every major AI accelerator is moving to N3 simultaneously in 2026, creating a capacity crunch. ## Why It's a Bottleneck - AI already consumes 60% of TSMC N3 output (2026), projected 86% by 2027 - N3 utilization at 100%+ in H2 2026 - TSMC Q4 2025 printed $33.73B revenue and 62.3% gross margin, then guided Q1 to $34.6B-$35.8B on strong leading-edge demand - TSMC 3nm already accounted for 28% of wafer revenue, while advanced...
title: N3 Wafer Crunch

text: NVDA Margin as Residual NVIDIA's gross margin is not a moat — it is a residual. The surplus left over after paying two supply constrained monopolists (TSMC for fabrication + packaging, the HBM oligopoly for memory) and before selling to four hyperscalers who are building their own alternatives. The margin compresses from both directions simultaneously. Th... # NVDA Margin as Residual NVIDIA's gross margin is not a moat — it is a residual. The surplus left over after paying two supply-constrained monopolists (TSMC for fabrication + packaging, the HBM oligopoly for memory) and before selling to four hyperscalers who are building their own alternatives. The margin compresses from both directions simultaneously. ## The Framework NVIDIA captures ~75% of the GPU selling price while contributing ~14% of the physical BoM. The gap is its vulnerability: ``` GPU selling price ($30-40K) minus TSM...

text: Neocloud Business Models Neoclouds should not be valued as one category. The label combines at least three businesses: full stack AI platforms, bare metal GPU clouds, and power landlords. They respond to the same AI infrastructure demand, but they do not own the same assets, earn the same margin pool, or carry the same risks. Model Split Model Examples Va... # Neocloud Business Models Neoclouds should not be valued as one category. The label combines at least three businesses: full-stack AI platforms, bare-metal GPU clouds, and power landlords. text: Token Economics (Tokconomics) Jensen Huang's framework (GTC26): computers are no longer tools, they are manufacturing equipment. Like ASML lithography machines or dynamos, they produce something (tokens) that is sold. The economics of AI infrastructure should be evaluated as factory economics, not IT procurement. The economics of AI infrastructure should be evaluated as factory economics, not IT procurement. ## Core Insight The price of the computer and the cost of the token are "only marginally related." text: Summary Cascade + Cycle Risk Positions + Signals Earnings + Proof Gates Drift Warnings
title: semi-stocks latest report

text: Cipher's $9.3B flagship leases show powered AI sites can be financed as contract-backed infrastructure.
title: CIFR

text: 600MW is contracted, but the longer 3.4GW pipeline remains exposed to ERCOT batch-study timing.
title: CIFR

text: Datacenter and communications was 72% of Q2 revenue ($1.208B) and management tied H2 FY2026 and FY2027 growth to continued datacenter demand plus production capacity expansion.
title: COHR

text: Q3 guide calls for $1.70B-$1.84B revenue and 38.5%-40.5% non-GAAP gross margin, implying AI-optics demand is supporting both growth and pricing/mix.
title: COHR

text: March 2026 NVIDIA partnership added a multibillion-dollar purchase commitment, future capacity rights for advanced laser and optical networking products, and a $2B NVIDIA investment to expand Coherent's U.S. manufacturing and R&D base.
title: COHR

text: OFC 2026 demos included 6.4T socketed silicon-photonics CPO, VCSEL-based socketed CPO, and a 400G-per-lane InP-on-silicon modulator path, showing Coherent has multiple technical routes into future package-level optics.
title: COHR

text: Core Scientific controls energized sites and leases powered capacity to CoreWeave while avoiding GPU ownership and refresh risk.
title: CORZ

text: CoreWeave's willingness to commit $10.2B over 12 years validates external hosting demand for scarce powered AI sites.
title: CORZ

text: AMD Venice: up to 256 cores, 1.7x perf/watt vs Turin; Intel Diamond Rapids: up to 256 cores, ~40% faster than Granite Rapids; Nvidia Vera: 2x performance over Grace, 6 dies; SoftBank acquired Ampere Computing for $6.5B; ARM datacenter royalty revenue more than doubled YoY, >1B Neoverse cores deployed
title: CPUs are Back: The Datacenter CPU Landscape in 2026

text: Revenue +168% YoY, backlog 4x'd to $66.8B, 'unable to catch up with demand signals', all capacity booked through Aug-Sep 2026
title: CRWV

text: Delivered 50K GB200s in Q4 to single customer. Nvidia GPUs 'remain in short supply' per Intrator. Massive capex plan ($30-35B) = enormous chip demand.
title: CRWV

text: Every GPU CRWV deploys needs HBM. $30-35B capex = substantial HBM demand. Storage ARR >$100M and growing.
title: CRWV

text: 850 MW active, 3.1 GW contracted, targeting 5+ GW by 2030. 43 data centers. 260 MW added in Q4 alone.
title: CRWV

text: Every NVL72 rack deployed uses pluggable optics for scale-out. 100K+ GPUs deployed in Q4 = significant transceiver demand.
title: CRWV

text: CPO = $20B market by 2036, 37% CAGR; 2026 is inflection year: prototypes → volume deployment; Required for Nvidia NVL576+ inter-rack connectivity; OFC panel: Nvidia, Broadcom, Marvell, AMD, Google, Meta, Oracle all present; TSMC SoIC 3D integration for CPO platforms
title: Co-Packaged Optics (CPO)

text: Helios shows that approved, utility-contracted power capacity can be converted into long-duration AI infrastructure offtake.
title: GLXY

text: Galaxy is not a pure-play landlord comp because digital-assets and capital-markets exposure remain material.
title: GLXY

text: Intel said AI-driven demand created meaningful supply constraints, DCAI revenue rose 15% sequentially, and revenue would have been materially higher with more supply.
title: INTC

text: Intel Foundry is ramping 18A, raising EUV wafer mix, and engaging external customers on 14A, which is precisely the kind of alternative leading-edge supply response that could ease the crunch later.
title: INTC

text: Intel flagged rising DRAM, NAND, and substrate pricing due to intense AI infrastructure demand, showing upstream component constraints are now feeding through into Intel's model.
title: INTC

text: Direct control of ~810MW operating high-density capacity and ~4.5GW secured grid-connected power makes IREN the strongest near-term deliverability case in the Creative Strategies ranking.
title: IREN

text: Bare-metal model monetizes owned GPUs but has less software differentiation than full-stack platforms.
title: IREN

text: Revenue rose 65.5% YoY, components were 66.7% of total revenue, and Q3 guide points to another major step up, showing AI optical demand is already moving the reported P&L.
title: LITE

text: OCS backlog is already above $400M, and the R64 OCS extends Lumentum's optical-circuit-switch lineup into lower-port-count AI deployments with 72x72 scaling and major power savings.
title: LITE

text: Management disclosed an incremental multi-hundred-million-dollar CPO order for 1H 2027 delivery, while NVIDIA added a $2B investment, a multibillion purchase commitment, and future capacity rights for advanced laser components.
title: LITE

text: Management said DRAM and NAND data-center bit TAM should exceed 50% of industry TAM in 2026 and that both AI and traditional server demand are constrained by inadequate supply.
title: MU

text: Record Q2 results with 74.9% non-GAAP gross margin and a Q3 guide of $33.5B revenue / ~81% gross margin show the shortage is converting directly into pricing and earnings power.
title: MU

text: Micron has begun HBM4 volume shipments for NVIDIA Vera Rubin and is investing above $25B in FY2026 capex, reinforcing that memory capacity is being pulled hard by the 2026 accelerator ramp.
title: MU

text: Meta's up-to-$27B commitment and large backlog validate external AI cloud demand beyond hyperscaler first-party footprints.
title: NBIS

text: Nebius has a 3GW target but only ~170MW active in late 2025, making greenfield deliverability and interconnection risk central.
title: NBIS

text: Rubin moving to N3, competing with TPU v7/v8, Trainium3, MI350X for TSMC capacity. $95.2B supply commitments (nearly doubled QoQ) = enormous wafer demand. Vera Rubin production H2 2026 adds further N3 pressure. Six new chips this cycle — all need N3.
title: NVDA

text: $21.4B inventory (significant HBM stockpile). Every GPU shipped needs HBM. NVIDIA GPU volumes are primary demand driver for memory supercycle. CRWV alone ordering $30-35B capex worth of GPUs = massive HBM pull-through.
title: NVDA

text: Networking revenue $11B (+3.5x YoY). Every NVL72 rack uses OSFP pluggable transceivers. Spectrum-X "a home run." "We ship racks of computers" = optics in every unit. Grace Blackwell = ~2/3 of DC revenue — all rack-scale with optics.
title: NVDA

text: Supply commitments doubled ($50.3B → $95.2B). $500B revenue visibility. Demand "growing exponentially." Validates CRWV thesis — NVIDIA can't build fast enough.
title: NVDA

text: Nvidia acquired Groq for ~$20B; Rubin Ultra NVL144: 144 GPUs/rack, 72 NVLink 7 switches, 28.8Tbit/s per switch; NVL576 (Rubin Ultra): CPO optics required between racks; CPO market projected $20B by 2036, 37% CAGR; Vera CPU: 88 cores, 1.5TB LPDDR5X, 1.8TB/s NVLink-C2C (2x Grace)
title: Nvidia — The Inference Kingdom Expands (GTC 2026)

text: HBM 85% less dense than DDR equivalent (0.16 vs 0.296 Gb/mm2); 12-layer HBM stack yield ~87% (at 99% per-layer); HBM market: $35B (2025) → $54.6B (2026); HBM bit share of DRAM: 19% (2025) → 23% (2026); China CXMT matching Micron TSV capacity by end 2025, ~2M stacks (enough for ~250K Ascend 910Cs); Rubin Ultra projected 1,024GB HBM4E per GPU
title: Scaling the Memory Wall: The Rise and Roadmap of HBM

text: Q4 revenue reached $33.73B with gross margin 62.3%, and management said both Q4 and Q1 2026 were supported by strong demand for leading-edge process technologies.
title: TSM

text: 3nm already accounted for 28% of wafer revenue, and 7nm-and-below advanced nodes were 77% of total wafer revenue, showing AI demand remains concentrated in constrained leading-edge supply.
title: TSM

text: A 2026 capital budget of $52B-$56B is a real supply response. It does not break the shortage today, but it is exactly the kind of capex that can eventually ease logic tightness.
title: TSM

text: AI = 60% of TSMC N3 output (2026), projected 86% by 2027; Reallocating 5% smartphone N3 wafers = ~100K Rubin GPUs or ~300K TPU v7s; Reallocating 25% = ~700K Rubin GPUs or ~1.5M TPU v7s; Google 2026 capex roughly doubled vs prior expectations; Anthropic added $6B ARR in Feb 2026 alone (Claude Code driven); HBM consumes ~3x more wafer capacity than commodity DRAM, widening to ~4x with HBM4; All major AI accelerators moving to N3 simultaneously: Rubin, TPU v7/v8, Trainium3, MI350X
title: The Great AI Silicon Shortage

text: H100 1yr rental: $1.70/hr (Oct 2025) → $2.35/hr (Mar 2026), +40%; AWS p6-b200 spot: $14/hr/GPU; Month-on-month rental increases 15-20% (Jan-Mar 2026); All new capacity through Aug-Sep 2026 already booked; Blackwell deployment lead times extended to Jun-Jul 2026; Anthropic ARR: $9B (end 2025) → $25B (early 2026)
title: The Great GPU Shortage — Rental Capacity

text: TeraWulf's Hawesville and Morgantown sites show why brownfield and BYOG strategies can be more valuable than announced greenfield MW.
title: WULF

text: Utilization-linked rent escalators make the landlord cash flow less purely fixed than a simple triple-net lease.
title: WULF

text: Wang draws LNG market parallel: when buyers shifted from spot to 15-20yr take-or-pay contracts, price volatility compressed and suppliers re-rated from cyclical to quasi-utility (Cheniere, Shell, TotalEnergies). HBM contracting trending same direction. If analogy holds, 5-8x fwd P/E on memory embeds unwarranted cyclical discount. SK Hynix 68.8% GM, Micron 74.9% GM — approaching/exceeding TSMC for first time in history.
title: thesis_proposal_signal

text: Ben Bajarin argues the neocloud category breaks into three different businesses: full-stack AI platforms (CoreWeave, Nebius), bare-metal GPU clouds (IREN), and power landlords (Core Scientific, Cipher Digital, TeraWulf). The article cites CoreWeave $66.8B backlog, Nebius/Meta up to $27B, IREN/Microsoft $9.7B, Core Scientific/CoreWeave $10.2B over 12 years, Cipher $9.3B leases, and revenue/MW spread between CoreWeave (~$10M-$12M) and Core Scientific (~$1.4M).
title: thesis_proposal_signal

text: Full report adds contract-quality and deliverability detail: IREN has $1.9B Microsoft prepayments and strongest near-term deliverability; Core Scientific has $755M CoreWeave-funded capex credits and strong energized-site position; Cipher has AWS and Google-backed lease structures but longer ERCOT queue exposure; TeraWulf has brownfield and generation advantages plus Google-backed Fluidstack economics; Galaxy Helios is an adjacent power-landlord example but not a pure-play comp.
title: thesis_proposal_signal

text: Wang's margin-as-residual framework. NVIDIA captures 75%+ of GPU ASP while contributing ~14% of BoM. Margin absorbs pressure from TSMC (5-10% annual hikes), HBM oligopoly (20%+ price increases), and hyperscaler custom silicon (TPU v7, Trainium2/3, Maia 200 as credible BATNA). GAAP GM already 75.0% → 71.1%. Street models 74% → 71% over 5yr, likely understating bilateral pressure magnitude.
title: thesis_proposal_signal

text: Wang ranks CoWoS as #1 in substitutability ranking (most constrained, least substitutable). CoWoS capacity ~75K wafers/month early 2026, targeting 130K by late 2026, but demand at 113% CAGR keeps it fully booked. TSMC at ~25x fwd P/E is a discount to S&P 500 IT (~32x) despite 92% sub-5nm share and packaging monopoly.
title: thesis_proposal_signal

text: Lumentum disclosed an incremental multi-hundred-million-dollar CPO order for 1H 2027 delivery
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Coherent's March 2026 NVIDIA partnership added multibillion-dollar purchase commitments, future capacity rights, and a $2B investment
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Coherent OFC 2026 demos included 6.4T silicon-photonics CPO, VCSEL socketed CPO, and a 400G-per-lane InP-on-silicon path
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Rubin Ultra NVL576: CPO between racks — explicitly low volume / test only
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Feynman NVL1152: CPO between racks, copper within rack (base case)
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: 448G SerDes challenges (shoreline, reach, power) favor CPO over copper at next speed step
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: ASML can produce ~100 EUV tools/yr by 2030 (Zeiss optics is the constraint)
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: One gigawatt of Rubin-class capacity needs ~3.5 EUV tools and ~2M EUV wafer passes
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Installed EUV base could approach ~700 tools by 2030, implying ~200 GW of annual AI-chip output if fully AI-allocated
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Near-term bottleneck is fab / cleanroom space; later bottleneck rotates to EUV tool throughput
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Micron Q2 FY2026: $23.86B revenue, 74.9% non-GAAP gross margin, and Q3 guide of ~$33.5B revenue / ~81% gross margin
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Micron says DRAM and NAND remain supply constrained beyond calendar 2026
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Micron began HBM4 volume shipments for NVIDIA Vera Rubin
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: DRAM contract prices +90-95% QoQ (Q1 2026, TrendForce)
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: DDR4 spot $2.10/Gbit — now exceeds HBM3e at $1.70/Gbit (unprecedented)
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: 30% of Big Tech $650B CapEx goes to memory
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: NVIDIA $21.4B inventory includes significant HBM stockpile (Q4 FY2026)
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: TSMC Q4 2025: $33.73B revenue, 62.3% gross margin, and Q1 guide of $34.6B-$35.8B on strong leading-edge demand
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: TSMC 3nm already accounts for 28% of wafer revenue, and advanced nodes are 77% of wafer revenue
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Intel said revenue would have been materially higher with more supply; DCAI revenue rose 15% sequentially
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: AI = 60% of TSMC N3 output (2026), projected 86% by 2027
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: N3 utilization 100%+ in H2 2026
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: All major AI accelerators moving to N3 simultaneously: Rubin, TPU v7/v8, Trainium3, MI350X
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: NVIDIA supply commitments doubled $50.3B → $95.2B QoQ, capacity secured through CY2027 (Q4 FY2026)
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Vera Rubin samples shipped, production H2 2026 — six new chips this cycle all on N3
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Coherent Q2 FY2026: datacenter and communications was 72% of revenue ($1.208B) and Q3 guide is $1.70B-$1.84B
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Lumentum Q2 FY2026: revenue grew 65.5% YoY and Q3 guide is $780M-$830M
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Lumentum optical-circuit-switch backlog is already above $400M
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Every NVL72/NVL144/NVL288 rack uses pluggable optics for scale-out networking
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: NVIDIA networking revenue $11B quarterly (+3.5x YoY), Spectrum-X 'a home run' (Q4 FY2026)
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: 56 GW of power announced; PJM backlog still 286 GW
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Power can route around bottlenecks through multiple lanes: combined-cycle turbines, aeroderivatives, reciprocating engines, ship engines, fuel cells, batteries, and grid peak-shaving
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Updated hierarchy: power remains hard, but the more durable choke points now look upstream in memory, wafers, fabs, and EUV tooling
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Neocloud model split: full-stack AI platforms capture higher revenue/MW but retain GPU refresh and financing risk; power landlords capture lower revenue/MW but may have cleaner duration because tenants absorb hardware obsolescence
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Deliverable power is now the relevant filter: energizable MW, interconnection rights, transformers, substations, permitting, skilled labor, and construction workflow matter more than announced MW
title: thesis_stage_signal

text: Lumentum disclosed an incremental multi-hundred-million-dollar CPO order for 1H 2027 delivery Coherent's March 2026 NVIDIA partnership added multibillion-dollar purchase commitments, future capacity rights, and a $2B investment Coherent OFC 2026 demos included 6.4T silicon-photonics CPO, VCSEL socketed CPO, and a 400G-per-lane InP-on-silicon path Rubin Ultra NVL576: CPO between racks — explicitly low volume / test only Feynman NVL1152: CPO between racks, copper within rack (base case) 448G SerDes challenges (shoreline, reach, power) favor CPO over copper at next speed step COHR and LITE now have real orders, partnerships, and demos, but revenue still points to 2027+ rather than a current bottleneck.
title: Co-packaged optics / CPO (scale-up)

text: ASML can produce ~100 EUV tools/yr by 2030 (Zeiss optics is the constraint) One gigawatt of Rubin-class capacity needs ~3.5 EUV tools and ~2M EUV wafer passes Installed EUV base could approach ~700 tools by 2030, implying ~200 GW of annual AI-chip output if fully AI-allocated Near-term bottleneck is fab / cleanroom space; later bottleneck rotates to EUV tool throughput ASML bookings hit record EUR 13.2B (Q4 2025), but the deeper issue is physical throughput: EUV and its supplier chain cap long-run AI-chip output.
title: EUV tools

text: Micron Q2 FY2026: $23.86B revenue, 74.9% non-GAAP gross margin, and Q3 guide of ~$33.5B revenue / ~81% gross margin Micron says DRAM and NAND remain supply constrained beyond calendar 2026 Micron began HBM4 volume shipments for NVIDIA Vera Rubin DRAM contract prices +90-95% QoQ (Q1 2026, TrendForce) DDR4 spot $2.10/Gbit — now exceeds HBM3e at $1.70/Gbit (unprecedented) 30% of Big Tech $650B CapEx goes to memory NVIDIA $21.4B inventory includes significant HBM stockpile (Q4 FY2026) Micron just printed a record quarter and guided ~81% gross margin, but Baker already hedged MU. Strong proof, late-cycle risk rising.
title: Memory supercycle

text: TSMC Q4 2025: $33.73B revenue, 62.3% gross margin, and Q1 guide of $34.6B-$35.8B on strong leading-edge demand TSMC 3nm already accounts for 28% of wafer revenue, and advanced nodes are 77% of wafer revenue Intel said revenue would have been materially higher with more supply; DCAI revenue rose 15% sequentially AI = 60% of TSMC N3 output (2026), projected 86% by 2027 N3 utilization 100%+ in H2 2026 All major AI accelerators moving to N3 simultaneously: Rubin, TPU v7/v8, Trainium3, MI350X NVIDIA supply commitments doubled $50.3B → $95.2B QoQ, capacity secured through CY2027 (Q4 FY2026) Vera Rubin samples shipped, production H2 2026 — six new chips this cycle all on N3 TSMC and Intel both confirmed AI-driven tightness, but TSMC's $52B-$56B capex and Intel's 18A/14A ramp are visible future relief valves.
title: N3 logic wafers

text: Coherent Q2 FY2026: datacenter and communications was 72% of revenue ($1.208B) and Q3 guide is $1.70B-$1.84B Lumentum Q2 FY2026: revenue grew 65.5% YoY and Q3 guide is $780M-$830M Lumentum optical-circuit-switch backlog is already above $400M Every NVL72/NVL144/NVL288 rack uses pluggable optics for scale-out networking NVIDIA networking revenue $11B quarterly (+3.5x YoY), Spectrum-X 'a home run' (Q4 FY2026) COHR and LITE are now printing direct AI-optics revenue, backlog, and guide data. Still early cycle, but consensus crowding is rising.
title: Pluggable optics (scale-out)

text: 56 GW of power announced; PJM backlog still 286 GW Power can route around bottlenecks through multiple lanes: combined-cycle turbines, aeroderivatives, reciprocating engines, ship engines, fuel cells, batteries, and grid peak-shaving Updated hierarchy: power remains hard, but the more durable choke points now look upstream in memory, wafers, fabs, and EUV tooling Neocloud model split: full-stack AI platforms capture higher revenue/MW but retain GPU refresh and financing risk; power landlords capture lower revenue/MW but may have cleaner duration because tenants absorb hardware obsolescence Deliverable power is now the relevant filter: energizable MW, interconnection rights, transformers, substations, permitting, skilled labor, and construction workflow matter more than announced MW Still monetizable, but increasingly substitution-rich: behind-the-meter engines, fuel cells, batteries,...
title: Power / DC buildout
